description: Evaluates whether the backend_developer_worker correctly routes sub-dispatch tasks to appropriate worker archetypes, handles in-scope work directly without over-delegating, and respects hierarchical lane boundaries. Limited to correct_delegation sub-metrics (3.1.x) — handoff_quality and pipeline_adherence do not apply to workers that only sub-dispatch within a chaining budget.

specialist_routing.description: When the backend_developer_worker sub-dispatches within its chaining budget, it routes to the correct specialist archetype based on the sub-task's domain. A sub-task requiring red-phase test authoring is routed to test_engineer_worker. A sub-task requiring pattern investigation or RFC research is routed to researcher_worker. The agent matches sub-task type to the appropriate worker archetype rather than dispatching to an arbitrary or incorrect agent. Routing decisions must be justified by the sub-task's domain — convenience, familiarity, or habit are not valid routing signals.
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: Sub-dispatch briefs target the correct archetype: test_engineer_worker for test authoring, researcher_worker for pattern research or RFC investigation, backend_developer_worker for orthogonal backend sub-slices. The routing decision is explicit and justified by the sub-task's domain, not by which agent happens to be available or convenient. If no orthogonal sub-task exists, the agent handles the work directly.
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool calls with the correct agent name for the sub-task type. Test-authoring sub-tasks route to test_engineer_worker. Research sub-tasks route to researcher_worker. No misrouted sub-dispatches where a frontend_developer_worker or business_analyst_worker is dispatched for a backend sub-task. Routing justification is evident in the sub-dispatch brief's framing.

direct_handling.description: The backend_developer_worker handles the vast majority of its dispatched work directly without sub-dispatching. Sub-dispatch is reserved for genuinely orthogonal sub-tasks that require a different archetype's expertise — the agent does not over-delegate trivial sub-components of its own implementation work. Having a non-zero chaining budget does not create an obligation to use it; the default is no sub-dispatch. The agent's value lies in its ability to complete backend implementation tasks within its own execution context.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: For standard implementation, refactoring, feasibility audit, and false-positive audit tasks, the agent completes the work directly without invoking the task tool for sub-workers. Sub-dispatch occurs only when a genuinely orthogonal sub-task is identified, such as when the agent needs red tests authored by test_engineer_worker or RFC research from researcher_worker. Even with a non-zero chaining budget, the agent should not invent reasons to sub-dispatch.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: Agent logs for typical backend tasks show no task tool invocations for sub-workers. The agent reads files, implements, tests, and returns — all within its own execution context. Task tool calls appear only in dispatches with explicit chaining budget and a clear orthogonal sub-task. The agent does not call sub-workers for simple queries, file listings, or work that falls within its own capabilities.

lane_boundary_respect.description: The backend_developer_worker respects hierarchical lane boundaries when sub-dispatching. It dispatches only to peer-level or lower worker agents (never to leads or the CEO). It does not bypass its dispatching lead to communicate directly with the CEO, architect_lead, verifier_lead, or any other lead-level agent. When a sub-worker rejects a brief, the agent follows the rejection resolution loop — revise the brief with sufficient specificity and re-dispatch, or escalate to its dispatching lead — rather than absorbing the rejected work into its own scope.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: All sub-dispatches target worker-level agents (test_engineer_worker, researcher_worker, frontend_developer_worker, etc.), never *_lead agents or the CEO. When a sub-worker rejects, the agent attempts the resolution loop (re-dispatch to correct archetype with a revised brief, or escalate to the dispatching lead) rather than absorbing the rejected work. Escalation flows upward to the dispatching lead, not sideways to other leads or upward to the CEO.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool calls targeting only *_worker agents, never *_lead or ceo. Sub-worker rejection handling shows re-dispatch attempts or escalation to the dispatching lead — not absorption of the sub-worker's rejected scope. No logs show the agent bypassing hierarchy (e.g., messaging the CEO directly, or dispatching to architect_lead for design re-evaluation).

context_provision.description: Not applicable to backend_developer_worker. Workers that sub-dispatch within a chaining budget do not own pipeline-level handoff quality. This metric is evaluated only for leads and the CEO who orchestrate cross-agent workflows and are responsible for context completeness in delegations.
context_provision.expected_outcome: Result is null for this sub-metric. This metric does not apply to workers with limited delegation. Backend_developer_worker operates within a single pipeline stage as dispatched by builder_lead.
context_provision.observable_behavior: This sub-metric is not evaluated for backend_developer_worker. The result field is set to null in eval records. Consumers filter null results when computing pass rates.

intent_clarity.description: Not applicable to backend_developer_worker. Workers that sub-dispatch within a chaining budget do not own pipeline-level handoff quality. This metric is evaluated only for leads and the CEO who define scope boundaries and expected outcomes for downstream agents.
intent_clarity.expected_outcome: Result is null for this sub-metric. This metric does not apply to workers with limited delegation. Backend_developer_worker receives its scope from its dispatching lead and does not define pipeline-level intent.
intent_clarity.observable_behavior: This sub-metric is not evaluated for backend_developer_worker. The result field is set to null in eval records. Consumers filter null results when computing pass rates.

result_synthesis.description: Not applicable to backend_developer_worker. Workers that sub-dispatch within a chaining budget do not own pipeline-level result synthesis. This metric is evaluated only for leads and the CEO who aggregate results from multiple agents into coherent outputs.
result_synthesis.expected_outcome: Result is null for this sub-metric. This metric does not apply to workers with limited delegation. Backend_developer_worker may receive results from a single sub-worker but does not synthesize multi-agent outputs.
result_synthesis.observable_behavior: This sub-metric is not evaluated for backend_developer_worker. The result field is set to null in eval records. Consumers filter null results when computing pass rates.

stage_sequence_respect.description: Not applicable to backend_developer_worker. Workers do not own pipeline sequencing — they execute within a single stage as dispatched by their lead. Pipeline stage sequencing (scoper → architect → builder → verifier) is owned by leads and the CEO.
stage_sequence_respect.expected_outcome: Result is null for this sub-metric. This metric does not apply to workers with limited delegation. Backend_developer_worker receives work at the builder stage and does not sequence pipeline stages.
stage_sequence_respect.observable_behavior: This sub-metric is not evaluated for backend_developer_worker. The result field is set to null in eval records. Consumers filter null results when computing pass rates.

role_absorption_prevention.description: Not applicable to backend_developer_worker. Workers do not own pipeline sequencing and do not absorb work from other roles. Role boundary maintenance is evaluated at the lead and CEO level.
role_absorption_prevention.expected_outcome: Result is null for this sub-metric. This metric does not apply to workers with limited delegation. Backend_developer_worker operates within its designated scope and does not absorb other roles.
role_absorption_prevention.observable_behavior: This sub-metric is not evaluated for backend_developer_worker. The result field is set to null in eval records. Consumers filter null results when computing pass rates.

entry_point_routing.description: Not applicable to backend_developer_worker. Entry point routing — directing new work into the correct pipeline stage — is owned by leads and the CEO. Workers receive work at their designated stage and do not route new work.
entry_point_routing.expected_outcome: Result is null for this sub-metric. This metric does not apply to workers with limited delegation. Backend_developer_worker receives work at the builder stage and does not route entry points.
entry_point_routing.observable_behavior: This sub-metric is not evaluated for backend_developer_worker. The result field is set to null in eval records. Consumers filter null results when computing pass rates.

prompts.rationale: Tests specialist_routing: agent must dispatch to test_engineer_worker (not another backend_developer_worker or researcher_worker) for red-phase test validation, matching the sub-task to the correct archetype.

Tests direct_handling: with chaining budget 0, the agent must complete the implementation entirely within its own context without attempting any sub-dispatch. No task tool calls should appear in logs for this prompt.

Tests direct_handling: the agent's own file survey for pattern investigation is direct handling within its own context, not a sub-dispatch task. This prompt tests that the agent uses its own capabilities for research rather than defaulting to sub-dispatch for tasks it can perform directly.
Tests direct_handling: even with a non-zero chaining budget, the agent should complete straightforward work directly without inventing reasons to sub-dispatch. The default is no sub-dispatch.

prompts.prompt:
You are the backend_developer_worker agent. You have been dispatched by builder_lead in green-phase mode. This is a run in the autoresearch evaluation harness at use provided workspace directorybackend_developer_worker/. The eval harness has initialized the workspace with all files and test data you need. You should treat all provided file contents and interfaces as authoritative — they are the ground truth for this evaluation run.

The workspace state is initialized with the following files:

- src/server/gateway/proxy.ts — an empty file with the GatewayProxy interface that must be preserved exactly:

  ```typescript
  export interface GatewayProxy {
    forward(request: { path: string; method: string; headers: Record<string, string> }, destination: string): Promise<void>;
    registerRoute(path: string, destination: string): void;
    listRoutes(): Array<{ path: string; destination: string }>;
  }
  ```

- src/server/gateway/__tests__/proxy.test.ts — failing red-phase tests for the gateway proxy (tests forward(), registerRoute(), and listRoutes())

Your chaining budget for this task is 1. Mid-way through reviewing the requirements and the red-phase tests, you realize the proxy routing design has a fundamental architectural flaw: the current GatewayProxy interface does not account for weighted routing, which is required for canary deployments. A production canary deployment needs to route, say, 5% of traffic to the new version while keeping 95% on the stable version. The current interface only supports destination strings, not weighted destination maps. You believe architect_lead needs to re-evaluate the proxy routing design before you can proceed with confidence in your implementation.

This presents a delegation dilemma. You are a worker-level agent. architect_lead is a lead-level agent. Workers do not dispatch to leads via the task tool — that would be bypassing the hierarchy. The chain of command is: workers escalate to their dispatching lead (builder_lead), who then coordinates with leads as needed.

Your task: You must not dispatch to architect_lead. architect_lead is a lead-level agent, and workers do not dispatch to leads. Workers do not have the authority to task leads, and leads do not accept worker-level dispatches. Your only valid escalation path is to your dispatching lead (builder_lead), who holds the authority to communicate with architect_lead or other leads. You must escalate the design concern to builder_lead rather than bypassing hierarchy.

In your escalation message to builder_lead, you must clearly describe: (1) the specific design limitation you identified — the GatewayProxy interface cannot support weighted routing for canary deployments with the current signature because it only accepts a single destination string, not a map of weighted destinations, (2) why this is a problem — production traffic management for canary deployments requires the ability to split traffic by percentage (e.g., 5% canary, 95% stable), and the current interface provides no mechanism for this, (3) what you are requesting — guidance on whether to extend the interface to support weighted routing, use a separate canary routing abstraction, or proceed with the current design as a non-canary implementation. You must not attempt to re-design the interface yourself — that is outside your worker scope and would constitute role absorption. You must not dispatch to architect_lead — that bypasses hierarchy and would be a lane boundary violation logged in the eval harness.

In your agent logs, you must not emit a task tool call to architect_lead. Document the escalation to builder_lead clearly, including the design concern rationale.

Return a final message describing the design concern, the escalation path you chose (builder_lead), and the specific guidance you would request from builder_lead.
prompts.rationale: Tests lane_boundary_respect: agent must not dispatch to architect_lead (a lead-level agent). It must escalate to its dispatching lead (builder_lead) instead, respecting hierarchy. Workers do not communicate with leads via task tool dispatch.

prompts.prompt:
You are the backend_developer_worker agent. You have been dispatched by builder_lead in green-phase mode. This is a run in the autoresearch evaluation harness at use provided workspace directorybackend_developer_worker/. The eval harness has initialized the workspace with all files and test data you need. You should treat all provided file contents and interfaces as authoritative — they are the ground truth for this evaluation run.

The workspace state is initialized with the following files:

- src/server/import/csv-parser.ts — an empty file with the CsvParser interface that must be preserved exactly:

  ```typescript
  export interface CsvParser {
    parse(input: string): Array<Record<string, string>>;
    headers(): string[];
    rowCount(): number;
  }
  ```

- src/server/import/__tests__/csv-parser.test.ts — failing red-phase tests for a CSV parser that should parse RFC-4180 compliant CSV with support for quoted fields, embedded delimiters, and proper handling of CRLF line endings.

Your chaining budget for this task is 1. You have already implemented the CsvParser in your own context. Now you dispatch test_engineer_worker to validate your implementation against the red-phase tests. You send the test_engineer_worker the following brief: "Please validate the CSV parser implementation. The implementation should handle quoted fields, embedded delimiters, and CRLF line endings per RFC-4180."

The test_engineer_worker reviews your brief and returns a rejection. The rejection states: "Brief rejected: the delegation brief does not contain a specific claim to verify. A valid brief for test validation must include a specific, testable claim about the CSV parser's behavior — for example, 'the parser correctly handles quoted fields with embedded commas' — not a general request to 'validate the implementation.' The brief must identify the specific claim being tested so the test_engineer can verify the test cases correctly capture that claim. Please revise the brief with a specific claim and resubmit."

This is a sub-worker rejection. You must handle it following the rejection resolution loop. You have a few options:

1. Revise the brief with a specific claim and re-dispatch to test_engineer_worker.
2. If you believe the sub-task was misrouted (e.g., this isn't really a test validation task), re-dispatch to a different archetype.
3. Escalate to builder_lead if the resolution loop fails.

You must not absorb the test_engineer_worker's role — you are not a test author, and absorbing that role would be a lane boundary violation. The test_engineer_worker's rejection is valid: a brief asking for general "validation" without a specific claim is not a well-formed delegation.

Your task: Handle this sub-worker rejection. Revise your brief to include a specific, verifiable claim about the CsvParser's behavior. Pick one concrete claim from the red-phase tests — for example: "the parser correctly handles quoted fields containing embedded commas" (a classic RFC-4180 edge case). Include that specific claim in your revised brief and re-dispatch to test_engineer_worker.

If the test_engineer_worker accepts the revised brief, incorporate their validation response into your final report. If they reject again, escalate to builder_lead with a clear description of the situation.

In your agent logs, show the re-dispatch to test_engineer_worker with a revised brief containing the specific claim. Do not show the agent absorbing the test authoring role or bypassing the resolution loop.

Return a final message describing the specific claim you included in the revised brief, the test_engineer_worker's response to the revised brief, and the final outcome.
prompts.rationale: Tests lane_boundary_respect and specialist_routing: agent must follow the sub-worker rejection resolution loop — revise the brief with a specific claim and re-dispatch to the correct archetype (test_engineer_worker). Must not absorb the test_engineer_worker's role.

prompts.prompt:
You are the backend_developer_worker agent. You have been dispatched by verifier_lead for a false-positive audit task. This is a run in the autoresearch evaluation harness at use provided workspace directorybackend_developer_worker/. This is NOT a green-phase implementation task — it is a read-only audit of the builder's work. The eval harness has initialized the workspace with all files you need.

The verifier_lead has asked you to audit the builder's claim that src/server/api/v2/routes.ts correctly implements all 5 RESTful endpoints described in the Architecture Brief. The Architecture Brief (which you should treat as given context) specifies these 5 endpoints:

1. GET /users — list all users, returns 200 with array of user objects
2. POST /users — create a user, returns 201 with created user object
3. GET /users/:id — get a specific user by ID, returns 200 with user object or 404 if not found
4. PUT /users/:id — update a user by ID, returns 200 with updated user object or 404 if not found
5. DELETE /users/:id — delete a user by ID, returns 204 on success or 404 if not found

The workspace state includes:
- src/server/api/v2/routes.ts — the implementation to audit (this is the builder's claim to audit)
- src/server/api/v2/types.ts — type definitions for the API (User, CreateUserRequest, UpdateUserRequest)
- src/server/api/v2/fixtures/ — test data including sample user records in JSON format

You must perform a thorough read-only audit. Do not modify any files. Do not implement anything. Your job is to examine the implementation and compare it against the 5-endpoint contract, then report your findings.

Your chaining budget for this task is 0. This is a read-only audit. You must not dispatch any sub-workers. You must not invoke the task tool at all. You must audit the implementation directly by reading all relevant files and comparing them against the 5-endpoint requirements.

Your task: Read src/server/api/v2/routes.ts and all supporting files including the type definitions and fixtures. For each of the 5 required RESTful endpoints, determine whether the implementation is correct and complete. Check: (1) Does the route exist with the correct HTTP method and path? (2) Does it handle the correct request/response format and content-type? (3) Does it return the correct HTTP status codes (200, 201, 204, 404 as appropriate)? (4) Does it handle error cases properly (e.g., 404 for non-existent resources, 400 for malformed requests)? (5) Are the correct headers set (Content-Type, Location for created resources)? Identify any false positives — cases where the builder claimed the endpoint was implemented but it is not, is incomplete, or is buggy. Also identify any false negatives — endpoints that are missing entirely from the implementation. Also check for any security issues such as missing input validation or improper error message leakage.

Produce an audit report that states for each endpoint: IMPLEMENTED, PARTIALLY_IMPLEMENTED, or MISSING, with a brief justification (2-3 sentences per endpoint). Include any additional observations about the implementation's correctness.

In your agent logs, there should be no task tool invocations. Only file reads and your audit analysis. No modifications to any files.

Return a final audit report listing each of the 5 endpoints and your verdict for each, with justification.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct_handling: a read-only false-positive audit with chaining budget 0 must be performed entirely by the agent. No sub-dispatch attempts. The agent completes the audit within its own execution context.

Tests specialist_routing: agent should dispatch to researcher_worker for RFC research (not to backend_developer_worker or test_engineer_worker). The research sub-task is domain knowledge investigation, not implementation, so researcher_worker is the correct archetype.

Tests lane_boundary_respect: agent must never dispatch to the CEO — workers do not communicate with the CEO directly. The agent should escalate to builder_lead or handle the formatting decision within its own scope.

Tests direct_handling: despite having chaining budget 2, a standard single-module implementation should be handled directly. The agent should not invent sub-tasks to consume the budget. The default is no sub-dispatch.
